schools.summary: The measuring symbol: 'and there was given me a reed like unto a rod (a measuring-reed): and the angel stood, saying, Rise, and measure the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein' — John given a measuring-reed and commanded to measure (the act, drawn from Ezekiel 40-43; Zechariah 2, signifying marking out, claiming, or preserving what is measured); measuring 'the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein' signifying the preservation and recognition of the true church and its true worshippers (in the symbolic reading the temple is the church, not the literal building likely already destroyed, the measuring setting apart and protecting the genuine people of God, distinguishing them from the false, v.2) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

Victorinus and Bede: the temple is the church; the measuring marks out the true worshippers within it, distinguishing the genuine people of God from the false (the outer court, v.2); the patristic/idealist reading takes the whole vision as the church's preservation and discrimination amid persecution, the true sanctuary measured and kept while the outer is given up.

themes: there was given me a reed like unto a rod... Rise, and measure the temple of God, and the altar, and them that worship therein
the command to measure the temple, the altar, and the worshippers
measuring as the preservation of the true church
the temple the church, the worshippers marked out

schools.summary: The unmeasured court and the trodden city: 'but the court which is without the temple leave out (cast out), and measure it not; for it is given unto the Gentiles (the nations): and the holy city shall they tread under foot forty and two months' — the outer court not measured but abandoned to the Gentiles (in the symbolic reading the outward, corrupted, or apostate part given over to the profane while the true sanctuary is preserved); 'forty and two months' (42 × 30 = 1260 days) read by Barnes and the historicist tradition by the day-year principle (Daniel 7:25; Ezekiel 4:6) as 1260 YEARS — the long period of the treading down of the church by the apostate/papal power (the same period as the 1260 days, v.3, the 'time, times and half a time,' and the 42 months elsewhere).

The preterist/literal reading (Stuart): the '42 months' a literal three years and a half — applied to the Roman siege and treading of Jerusalem (though, as Barnes notes, the wars of the Romans were not confined to exactly 3.5 years), or to a literal short period; the futurist takes it as a literal final 3.5 years of the church's oppression under the last antichrist. The number divides between the symbolic day-year (1260 years) and the literal 3.5 years.

debates.question: Is the 'forty-two months' (1260 days) a literal three and a half years or, by the day-year principle, 1260 years?

sides.position: 1260 years — the day-year principle (Daniel 7:25), the long oppression of the church by the apostate/papal power (historicist)
A literal three and a half years — the Roman treading of Jerusalem (preterist) or a final period under antichrist (futurist)

themes: the court which is without the temple leave out... for it is given unto the Gentiles
the holy city shall they tread under foot forty and two months
the outer court given to the Gentiles and the city trodden forty-two months
the crux of the prophetic period (1260 years vs 3.5 years)

schools.summary: Gill (with the historicist tradition): the 'two witnesses' are not literal individuals but the faithful ministers and churches who witnessed for Christ and against antichrist through the long apostasy — the Waldenses and Piedmont churches especially fitting the character (on the spot when antichrist arose, always protesting against Rome, independent of it, suffering great persecution yet standing). They prophesy 1260 days (the same period as the 42 months, read as 1260 years) in sackcloth (marking the mourning and affliction of their testimony during the church's long oppression), few yet sufficient witnesses (in the mouth of two every cause is established).

The patristic/futurist reading (noted by Gill, held by 'the ancient fathers'): the two witnesses are two literal prophets — Enoch and Elijah (caught up without death, to return before the end), or Moses and Elijah — who will come before Christ's appearance, oppose antichrist, be slain by him, rise, and ascend; the signs of vv.5-6 (fire from the mouth, shutting heaven so it rains not, turning water to blood, smiting with plagues) echoing Elijah and Moses. Gill notes the Papists 'greedily catch at' the Enoch-Elijah view.

A third view (noted and rejected by Gill): the two witnesses are the two Testaments, Old and New — their name, number (two), and office (to testify of Christ) agreeing, but the sackcloth, being slain, rising, and ascending not fitting the Scriptures. Barnes and the symbolic interpreters take the witnesses as the true witnessing church/ministry maintained through the period of oppression, clothed in the sackcloth of affliction.

sides.position: The faithful witnessing church/ministers against antichrist (the Waldenses) through the 1260-year period (historicist)
Two literal prophets — Enoch and Elijah, or Moses and Elijah — returning to oppose antichrist before the end (patristic/futurist)
The two Testaments, Old and New, witnessing to Christ
holders: some interpreters (noted and rejected by Gill)

themes: I will give power unto my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy a thousand two hundred and threescore days, clothed in sackcloth
the two witnesses prophesying 1260 days in sackcloth
the crux of the witnesses' identity
the witnessing church, or Enoch and Elijah, or the two Testaments

schools.summary: The figure from Zechariah: 'these are the two olive trees, and the two candlesticks (lampstands) standing before the God of the earth' — the witnesses identified with the two olive trees and two lampstands of Zechariah's vision (Zechariah 4), the figure marking them as bearers and channels of light and the Spirit; in Zechariah the two olive trees supply oil to the lampstand, so the witnesses, as olive trees, are filled with and supply the oil of the Spirit (their testimony sustained by the Spirit's grace, not their own strength, 'not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit,' Zechariah 4:6); and as lampstands they hold forth the light of truth before God (the witnessing church/ministers shining as lights in the dark time of apostasy, standing in God's presence) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: These are the two olive trees, and the two candlesticks standing before the God of the earth
the witnesses as the two olive trees and candlesticks
filled with the oil of the Spirit and bearing the light of truth
the figure from Zechariah's vision

schools.summary: The defensive judgment: 'and if any man will hurt them, fire proceedeth out of their mouth, and devoureth their enemies: and if any man will hurt them, he must in this manner be killed' — the witnesses divinely protected, any who try to harm them consumed by fire from their mouth (the fire the figure of the word of judgment, cf. Jeremiah 5:14, 'I will make my words in thy mouth fire'; 2 Kings 1:10, Elijah's fire from heaven, that destroys their foes); 'fire out of their mouth' the judicial power of their testimony (their word bringing judgment on persecutors, whether literal fire in the Elijah-figure or the consuming power of the word and the judgments it calls down, the witnesses not defenseless); the doom of their persecutors certain, the witnesses inviolable during their appointed testimony, unable to be silenced until their work is done (v.7) — Barnes, Robertson, JFB.

themes: if any man will hurt them, fire proceedeth out of their mouth, and devoureth their enemies
he must in this manner be killed
the witnesses' power to consume their enemies with fire
the word of judgment guarding the inviolable witnesses

schools.summary: The prophetic signs: 'these have power to shut heaven, that it rain not in the days of their prophecy: and have power over waters to turn them to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues, as often as they will' — the witnesses wielding the powers of Elijah (shutting heaven so it rains not, 1 Kings 17; James 5:17; Luke 4:25, Elijah's withholding of rain for three and a half years, the same period as the witnesses' testimony) and Moses (turning water to blood and smiting with plagues, Exodus 7-12, the plagues on Egypt); the figure of prophets armed with divine, judicial power to bring judgment on the persecuting world 'as often as they will' — like Moses and Elijah, armed against the ungodly (these Elijah/Moses signs being a chief support for the patristic identification of the witnesses, 11:3) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: these have power to shut heaven, that it rain not in the days of their prophecy
power over waters to turn them to blood, and to smite the earth with all plagues
the witnesses' Elijah-like and Moses-like powers
prophets armed with divine judicial power

schools.summary: The completion and the beast's war: 'and when they shall have finished (completed) their testimony, the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit (the abyss) shall make war against them, and shall overcome them, and kill them' — the witnesses unable to be killed until their testimony is finished (their inviolability, vv.5-6, lasting until the end of their commission, death coming only when their appointed work is done); 'the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit' the first mention of 'the beast,' the great persecuting power (later described in chs. 13, 17, in the historicist reading the antichristian/papal power), making war against the witnesses (the organized hostility of the persecuting power against the faithful); the beast overcoming and slaying them (the witnesses at last overcome, the testimony for a time silenced and seemingly defeated, the temporary triumph of the persecuting power over the witnessing church) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: when they shall have finished their testimony, the beast that ascendeth out of the bottomless pit shall make war against them
shall overcome them, and kill them
the beast from the abyss making war on the witnesses and killing them
the temporary triumph of the persecuting power

schools.summary: The unburied corpses and the symbolic city: 'and their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city, which spiritually is called Sodom and Egypt, where also our Lord was crucified' — the slain witnesses left unburied, exposed in the public street (the refusal of burial, v.9, the height of contempt and triumph over them); 'the great city, which spiritually (pneumatikos — figuratively, allegorically) is called Sodom and Egypt' named not literally but spiritually — 'Sodom' for its wickedness and immorality, 'Egypt' for its oppression and bondage of God's people (the apostate, persecuting city/power meant, the historicists reading it of Rome, the great city of ch. 17, the seat of the antichristian power); 'where also our Lord was crucified' most naturally recalling Jerusalem, but on the symbolic/historicist reading the city/power that is in spirit like the Jerusalem that crucified Christ — the persecuting power that crucifies Christ afresh in his members (Barnes, Robertson, Gill, Ellicott).

themes: their dead bodies shall lie in the street of the great city, which spiritually is called Sodom and Egypt
where also our Lord was crucified
the witnesses' bodies in the street of the great city
the apostate persecuting power named Sodom and Egypt

schools.summary: The public exposure: 'and they of the people and kindreds and tongues and nations shall see their dead bodies three days and an half, and shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves' — peoples from all nations looking upon the unburied corpses for three and a half days (the slain witnesses exposed to the gaze of the whole hostile world, the fourfold designation marking the universal scope of the spectacle); 'three days and an half' the period of the witnesses' apparent death (a short, limited time, in the historicist reading by the day-year principle three and a half years, the short duration of their seeming defeat); 'shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves' — denying burial the ultimate insult and triumph (the refusal of a grave expressing the malice and exultation of the persecutors over the silenced witnesses, the bodies left exposed in scorn) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they of the people and kindreds and tongues and nations shall see their dead bodies three days and an half
shall not suffer their dead bodies to be put in graves
the witnesses' bodies viewed and denied burial three and a half days
the contempt and triumph of the persecutors

schools.summary: The world's exultation: 'and they that dwell upon the earth shall rejoice over them, and make merry, and shall send gifts one to another; because these two prophets tormented them that dwelt on the earth' — the earth-dwellers (the ungodly world) rejoicing and celebrating over the death of the witnesses, exchanging gifts as in a festival (the world glad to be rid of the troublesome witnesses); the exchange of gifts marking a time of public rejoicing and festival (cf. Esther 9:22; Nehemiah 8:10, the world treating the witnesses' death as cause for celebration, the contrast between the world's joy and the witnesses' apparent defeat heightening the irony before their resurrection); the world rejoicing because the witnesses' testimony had been a torment to the ungodly (their faithful witness, rebuke, and the judgments they called down vexing the world's conscience, the world relieved at their silencing) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: they that dwell upon the earth shall rejoice over them, and make merry, and shall send gifts one to another
because these two prophets tormented them that dwelt on the earth
the world's rejoicing over the dead witnesses
the ungodly glad to be rid of the tormenting testimony

schools.summary: The resurrection of the witnesses: 'and after three days and an half the Spirit (pneuma — breath) of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet; and great fear fell upon them which saw them' — when the brief period of their death ends, the Spirit of life from God entering the slain witnesses (echoing Ezekiel 37:10, the breath entering the dry bones), they raised and standing on their feet (the testimony, seemingly dead, revived by God's power); the raising God's act ('the Spirit of life from God'), not human (the witnessing church/testimony, which the world thought destroyed, restored to life by God, in the historicist reading the revival of the witnessing church, e.g. at the Reformation); 'great fear fell upon them which saw them' — the world's rejoicing turning to terror at the sight of the revived witnesses (the same enemies who exulted now trembling, the reversal striking fear into the persecutors) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: after three days and an half the Spirit of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet
great fear fell upon them which saw them
the witnesses raised by the Spirit of life
the testimony revived, the world's triumph turned to terror

schools.summary: The ascension and vindication: 'and they heard a great voice from heaven saying unto them, Come up hither. And they ascended up to heaven in a cloud; and their enemies beheld them' — the witnesses, raised, summoned by a heavenly voice to ascend, going up to heaven in a cloud (as Christ ascended, Acts 1:9; as Elijah was taken up, 2 Kings 2:11, the full vindication and exaltation of the witnesses); 'Come up hither' the summons to glory (the witnesses not merely revived but exalted to heaven, their reward and triumph after their testimony and death, the seemingly defeated openly honored); 'their enemies beheld them' the public vindication (the very persecutors who exulted over their death now witnessing their triumphant exaltation, the open vindication before those who had despised them, the reversal complete and public) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they heard a great voice from heaven saying unto them, Come up hither. And they ascended up to heaven in a cloud
their enemies beheld them
the witnesses called up to heaven before their enemies
the full and public vindication of the testimony

schools.summary: The judgment and the remnant's response: 'and the same hour was there a great earthquake, and the tenth part of the city fell, and in the earthquake were slain of men seven thousand (literally, seven thousand names of men): and the remnant were affrighted, and gave glory to the God of heaven' — at the moment of the witnesses' vindication a great earthquake striking and a tenth of the great city collapsing (the figure of a great judgment or revolution shaking the persecuting power, in the historicist reading a political/religious convulsion); 'seven thousand' slain a definite number (whether literal or symbolic, a notable destruction, the judgment falling on the city that triumphed over the witnesses); and the survivors terrified and giving glory to the God of heaven (whether in true repentance or forced acknowledgment, brought to own God's hand, a notable, hopeful result — some turned to give God glory, in contrast to 9:20-21 where none repented) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: a great earthquake, and the tenth part of the city fell, and in the earthquake were slain of men seven thousand
the remnant were affrighted, and gave glory to the God of heaven
the earthquake and the remnant giving glory to God
the persecuting power shaken and survivors humbled

schools.summary: The structuring announcement (as at 9:12): 'the second woe is past; and, behold, the third woe cometh quickly' — the sixth trumpet (the second woe, including the Euphratean horsemen and the interlude of the witnesses) now past, the third woe (the seventh trumpet) announced as coming quickly (the marker between the trumpets); 'the second woe is past' closing the long section of the sixth trumpet (chs. 9-11), the second of the three woe-trumpets (8:13) having run its course; 'the third woe cometh quickly' the seventh trumpet, the third and final woe, now near ('quickly' marking its swift approach), yet the seventh trumpet when it sounds (v.15) bringing not only woe but the consummation and the kingdom of Christ — the final woe and the final triumph bound together (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: The second woe is past; and, behold, the third woe cometh quickly
the passing of the second woe and the coming of the third
the marker before the seventh and final trumpet
the final woe and the kingdom bound together

schools.summary: The climactic seventh trumpet: 'and the seventh angel sounded; and there were great (loud) voices in heaven, saying, The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ; and he shall reign for ever and ever' — the seventh and final trumpet sounding, heavenly voices proclaiming the great consummation (the third woe also the announcement of the triumph of God's kingdom); 'the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ' (some texts 'the kingdom of the world is become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ') — the sovereignty over the world passed to God and his Christ (the long-contested dominion now Christ's, the goal of the whole book proleptically declared, the universal reign of Christ); 'he shall reign for ever and ever' the everlasting reign of Christ (or of God), the kingdom now established never ending, the consummation of all things in the unending reign of the Lord and his Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the seventh angel sounded; and there were great voices in heaven
The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ; and he shall reign for ever and ever
the seventh trumpet and the kingdoms of the world becoming Christ's
the universal, everlasting reign of Christ

schools.summary: The response of heaven: 'and the four and twenty elders, which sat before God on their seats (thrones), fell upon their faces, and worshipped God' — the twenty-four elders (representing the redeemed church, introduced in ch. 4, the heavenly representatives of God's people, perhaps the twelve patriarchs and twelve apostles, or the whole redeemed church, who sit on thrones before God sharing his reign) responding to the proclamation of Christ's kingdom by falling on their faces in worship; 'fell upon their faces, and worshipped God' the lowliest posture of reverence and praise (at the announcement of the kingdom, heaven responding not with self-congratulation but with humble worship of God, the consummation calling forth adoration) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry.

themes: the four and twenty elders, which sat before God on their seats, fell upon their faces, and worshipped God
the twenty-four elders worshipping God at the proclamation
the redeemed church adoring God for his kingdom
the consummation calling forth worship

schools.summary: The elders' thanksgiving: 'saying, We give thee thanks, O Lord God Almighty, which art, and wast, and art to come; because thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned (begun to reign)' — the elders giving thanks to the eternal, almighty God (the title from 1:4, 8), praising the everliving God for the establishment of his reign; 'thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned' — God now openly assuming his great power and beginning to reign (the kingdom long awaited now established, God who always reigned in providence now reigning manifestly and triumphantly over the world, the consummation of his kingdom); many texts reading only 'which art, and wast' (omitting 'art to come') — fittingly, for at the consummation God is no longer the 'coming' one but the one who has now come and reigns, the reign so long future now present (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: We give thee thanks, O Lord God Almighty, which art, and wast
thou hast taken to thee thy great power, and hast reigned
the elders' thanksgiving that God has taken his power and reigned
the kingdom long awaited now established

schools.summary: The consummation's content: 'and the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come, and the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and that thou shouldest give reward unto thy servants the prophets, and to the saints, and them that fear thy name, small and great; and shouldest destroy them which destroy the earth' — the nations raging against God's reign but God's wrath now come (the angry rebellion of the nations met by the righteous wrath of God, the time of reckoning, echoing Psalm 2:1-5); the seventh trumpet bringing the time of judging the dead (the general judgment) and rewarding God's faithful servants (the prophets, saints, and all who fear God, great and small); and the time for God to destroy those who destroy/corrupt/ruin the earth (the persecutors, corrupters, and oppressors, the just retribution on the enemies of God and his people) — the kingdom's coming bringing both reward for the faithful and ruin for the destroyers (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Henry).

themes: the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come, and the time of the dead, that they should be judged
give reward unto thy servants the prophets, and to the saints... and shouldest destroy them which destroy the earth
the time of wrath, judgment, reward, and the destruction of the destroyers
the full content of the seventh trumpet

schools.summary: The opened heavenly temple: 'and the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament: and there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail' — the heavenly temple opened, revealing the ark of the covenant within (the ark, the symbol of God's covenant presence and faithfulness, hidden in the earthly Holy of Holies, now disclosed in heaven, the figure of God's covenant mercy and presence made manifest at the consummation); 'the ark of his testament (covenant)' signifying God's covenant with his people (his faithfulness, mercy, and the sure fulfillment of his promises, the opening of the temple and the seeing of the ark assuring the redeemed of God's covenant kept, the long-hidden ark revealed marking the access and faithfulness of God); accompanied by 'lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail' — the tokens of divine majesty and judgment (cf. Exodus 19; Revelation 8:5; 16:18), the awe-inspiring signs attending God's presence and the consummating judgment (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the temple of God was opened in heaven, and there was seen in his temple the ark of his testament
there were lightnings, and voices, and thunderings, and an earthquake, and great hail
the heavenly temple opened, revealing the ark, with mighty signs
God's covenant faithfulness revealed amid the tokens of his majesty
